claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started, I need to ask you some questions about your situation.

**First, let me ask about the child you're claiming for:**

**How old is your child?**

The age of the child is important because:
- Children **under 16** generally qualify automatically (subject to other conditions)
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** don't qualify for Child Benefit

Please tell me your child's age, and if you have more than one child, we can go through each of them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I have two children actually - Alex who is 17, and Blake who just turned 18. They both live at home with me. Should I go through them one at a time or tell you about both?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's great that you have the details ready. Yes, let's go through each child one at a time to make sure we assess their eligibility properly.

**Let's start with Alex, who is 17.**

Since Alex is between 16 and 19 years old, their eligibility will depend on whether they're in approved education or training.

**Is Alex currently enrolled in approved education or training?**

This includes:
- Full-time non-advanced education (such as A-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQ level 3 or below)
- Approved training programs (like certain vocational training)

The education/training must be full-time and approved by HMRC.

Is Alex in this type of education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is studying for A-levels at the local sixth form college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! A-levels at a sixth form college count as approved full-time non-advanced education, so Alex meets that requirement.

Let me continue with the assessment for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! A-levels are on the approved list. Now I need to check a few more things about Alex's situation:

**Does Alex work at all?** 

Specifically, I need to know if Alex works 24 hours or more per week. Young people aged 16-19 in approved education can still qualify for Child Benefit even if they work part-time, but not if they're working full-time (24+ hours per week).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all - just focuses on the studies.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That's good for the eligibility. Let me continue checking the requirements for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now, another important question about Alex:

**Is Alex receiving any benefits in their own right?**

Specifically, benefits like:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit
- Tax credits

If Alex is receiving these types of benefits in their own name, they wouldn't qualify for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't get any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with the assessment for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
One more question about Alex's education:

**Is Alex doing an apprenticeship?** Or is it just regular A-level study at sixth form college?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, it's just regular A-level study at the sixth form college, not an apprenticeship.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Now let me move on to questions about **you** as the claimant, which will apply to both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to ask about your residency status:

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

This is a requirement for claiming Child Benefit. There are also some special circumstances that can qualify you (such as being a Crown servant abroad, or having certain immigration statuses), but the most common route is simply living in the UK normally.

Do you normally live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Living in Manchester definitely satisfies the UK residency requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now, regarding your responsibility for the children:

**You mentioned both Alex and Blake live at home with you - is that correct?**

Living with the children is one of the main ways to show you're responsible for them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - they both live at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Living with both children satisfies the responsibility requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now an important question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

For example:
- Another parent or partner
- A former partner
- Another family member

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for either of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about the children's living situation:

**Are either Alex or Blake currently:**
- In hospital (for a stay longer than a few weeks)?
- In local authority care?
- Being fostered?

Or are they just living normally at home with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They're just living normally at home with me - nothing like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
One final question:

**Is the local council paying towards either Alex or Blake's accommodation or maintenance costs?**

This would be something like the council paying for their housing or living expenses.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Now let me check on **Blake, who is 18**. I need to go through similar questions for Blake to assess their eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! That completes the assessment for Alex. Based on Alex being 17, studying A-levels full-time, not working, and meeting all other requirements, **Alex qualifies for Child Benefit**.

Now let's assess **Blake, who is 18 years old**.

**Is Blake currently enrolled in approved education or training?**

This would include:
- Full-time non-advanced education (such as A-levels, T-levels, NVQ level 3 or below)
- Approved training programs

Is Blake in this type of education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't in education or training anymore.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. Let me ask a follow-up question:

**When did Blake finish their education or training?**

If Blake left education or training recently (within the last 20 weeks), there may be an extension period during which they could still qualify for Child Benefit. This extension can cover the time between leaving education and either:
- Starting work
- Registering for work or benefits
- The end of the extension period

Did Blake leave education within the last 20 weeks, or was it longer ago than that?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It was longer ago than that - Blake finished school a while back now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand. Let me ask one more important question about Blake:

**Is Blake working at all?** And if so, how many hours per week?

For young people aged 18-19 who are not in education or training, if they're working 24 hours or more per week, they would not qualify for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't work.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me now assess the complete situation for both of your children.

Based on what you've told me:

**For Alex (17 years old):**
- Studying A-levels full-time at sixth form college ✓
- Not working ✓
- Not receiving benefits in their own right ✓
- Living at home with you ✓
- All other requirements met ✓

**For Blake (18 years old):**
- Not in education or training
- Left education more than 20 weeks ago (so no extension period applies)
- Not working
- Living at home with you

Unfortunately, for young people aged 16-19, they must be in **approved education or training** to qualify for Child Benefit (unless they only recently left within the last 20 weeks). Since Blake is 18, not in education or training, and left education more than 20 weeks ago, Blake does not meet the qualifying child criteria - even though Blake is not working.

Let me provide you with the formal assessment:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment Results

### **Alex (17 years old): ✓ ELIGIBLE**

Good news! You **are eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Alex because:
- Alex is studying A-levels full-time, which is approved non-advanced education
- Alex is not working full-time (under 24 hours per week)
- Alex is not receiving benefits in their own right
- All other requirements are met

### **Blake (18 years old): ✗ NOT ELIGIBLE**

Unfortunately, you are **not eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Blake because:
- Blake is not currently in approved education or training
- Blake left education more than 20 weeks ago (the extension period has expired)
- For young people aged 16-19, they must be in approved education or training to qualify for Child Benefit

---

## Next Steps for Alex:

You can claim Child Benefit for Alex by:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** - if you or your partner earn over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay some or all of the benefit back through tax
5. **Payments** are usually made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

**Apply here:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

---

## Information about Blake:

If Blake decides to return to education or training in the future (such as enrolling in college, A-levels, T-levels, or approved training), you could become eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake again, as long as Blake is still under 20 when the course starts.

Do you have any questions about this assessment or the next steps?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and studying A-levels full-time at sixth form college, which qualifies as approved non-advanced education. Alex is not working (less than 24 hours per week), not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives at home with the claimant in the UK, no other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. Alex meets all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

Blake is 18 years old and is not currently enrolled in approved education or training. Blake left education more than 20 weeks ago, so the extension period (which allows up to 20 weeks after leaving education) does not apply. Under UK Child Benefit rules, young people aged 16-19 must be in approved education or training to qualify as a qualifying child, unless they left education very recently (within 20 weeks). Since Blake does not meet this requirement, Blake does not qualify as a qualifying child for Child Benefit purposes, regardless of employment status.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in Manchester (UK residency requirement met), is responsible for both children who live at home, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for either child. Alex (17) qualifies because they are in approved full-time education (A-levels). Blake (18) does not qualify because they are not in approved education or training and left education more than 20 weeks ago, which means they no longer meet the qualifying child criteria for the 16-19 age group.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements

Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old and studying A-levels full-time at sixth form college, which qualifies as approved non-advanced education. Alex is not working (less than 24 hours per week), not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives at home with the claimant in the UK, no other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. Alex meets all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

Blake is 18 years old and is not currently enrolled in approved education or training. Blake left education more than 20 weeks ago, so the extension period (which allows up to 20 weeks after leaving education) does not apply. Under UK Child Benefit rules, young people aged 16-19 must be in approved education or training to qualify as a qualifying child, unless they left education very recently (within 20 weeks). Since Blake does not meet this requirement, Blake does not qualify as a qualifying child for Child Benefit purposes, regardless of employment status.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in Manchester (UK residency requirement met), is responsible for both children who live at home, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for either child. Alex (17) qualifies because they are in approved full-time education (A-levels). Blake (18) does not qualify because they are not in approved education or training and left education more than 20 weeks ago, which means they no longer meet the qualifying child criteria for the 16-19 age group.
